content.text: A “GovTranslate pattern” for calling the Claude API from a Chrome extension usually means a
privacy-first, no-backend architecture

: the extension gathers page context in a content script, sends it to the background service worker, and the service worker makes the direct HTTPS request to Anthropic using a user-provided API key. This pattern keeps secrets out of page context, avoids routing user data through the developer’s server, and fits Manifest V3’s separation between content scripts and background logic.

attrs.alt: Calling Claude API from a Chrome extension (GovTranslate pattern) diagram

content.text: What it is
In Chrome extension architecture, the safest place to call the Claude API is typically the
background service worker

, not the content script. Content scripts run alongside web pages and interact with the DOM, while the service worker is the extension-controlled layer that can mediate secrets, storage, and network calls.

This architecture is especially useful for translation, summarization, rewriting, and extraction tools. A content script captures the selected text or page content, the background service worker reads the user’s Anthropic API key from extension storage, sends the request to
api.anthropic.com
, and returns the result to the UI or the page.
Why it matters
The main reason for this pattern is
security

. If the API key is exposed to the content script, it sits closer to untrusted webpage context, whereas the background service worker provides a more controlled environment for secret handling and external API access.
The second reason is
privacy

. Several extension architectures now deliberately avoid a custom backend so that prompts and page text travel directly from the browser to Anthropic, with no intermediary server operated by the extension developer. That reduces data handling obligations and user trust concerns.
A third reason is feasibility: Anthropic enabled browser-based API requests with CORS support when the request includes the
anthropic-dangerous-direct-browser-access: true
header, which makes direct extension-to-Anthropic calls possible. Simon Willison’s write-up highlights this as the change that unlocked true client-side Claude integrations.
Mental model

Think of the extension as a three-step relay:
- Content script: collect the text from the page.
- Background service worker: own the API key and call Claude.
- Popup, side panel, or injected UI: display the result.

That separation matters because each piece has a different trust boundary. The page-facing code should gather context, the extension-core code should handle credentials and network access, and the UI layer should remain a thin presentation surface.
How it works

The GovTranslate-style flow usually looks like this:
1. A user highlights text or activates the extension on a page.
2. The content script extracts the selected text, main article text, or a DOM fragment and sends it to the background service worker.
3. The service worker reads the Anthropic API key from
chrome.storage.sync
or
chrome.storage.local

.
4. The service worker sends a direct HTTPS request to Anthropic’s API endpoint.
5. The service worker returns the Claude response to the content script, popup, or side panel for rendering.

The privacy-first variant explicitly avoids developer-hosted infrastructure. One published architecture describes the path as: highlight text, message the service worker, service worker loads the key from browser storage, direct request to
api.anthropic.com
, then response back to the page UI.
Core architecture
A clean implementation usually maps to these files:
Component
Role
manifest.json
Declares permissions, service worker, content scripts, popup or side panel.
content.js
Extracts selected text or page content and sends it to background.
background.js
or service worker
Reads API key from storage and calls Claude.
popup.js
or
sidepanel.js
Lets the user enter API key, prompt settings, and view results.

A typical message flow is:
-
content.js
sends
{ type: "TRANSLATE", text }

.
- The service worker handles the message, loads the API key, calls Anthropic, then returns
{ result }
Example implementation
A minimal Manifest V3 setup would look like this:
The content script can gather text and send it to the service worker:
The service worker can mediate the Claude call:

The key browser-specific detail is that direct Claude API access from the browser depends on the special CORS-enabling header noted above. Without it, the extension may hit browser-origin restrictions.
Benefits

This pattern has several strong advantages:
-
No backend required

, which simplifies deployment and reduces infrastructure cost.
-
Better privacy posture

, since text is sent directly from the browser to Anthropic.
-
Cleaner secret boundaries

, because the key lives in extension storage and is used only in the service worker.
-
Good fit for MV3
, where background logic is already centered around a service worker.

It also scales well from translation to summarization, explanation, rewriting, classification, extraction, and on-page assistant features. The same architecture works whether the extension shows results in a popup, tooltip, or side panel.
Trade-offs
The biggest trade-off is that the
user usually supplies their own API key
. That is good for privacy and cost transparency, but it adds onboarding friction and support burden.
ui

Another trade-off is service worker lifecycle complexity. Manifest V3 service workers are event-driven and can be suspended, so long-running streaming interactions may require ports or moving the UI logic to a longer-lived popup or side panel. Some extension guides specifically recommend long-lived connections or side panels to handle richer streaming responses.

There is also a risk surface around direct browser access. The special CORS header is intentionally named as a warning, because direct browser calls expose a different trust model than server-side calls. That means key handling, UI permissions, and prompt scoping must be designed carefully.
Security and reliability

Best practices for this pattern include:
- Store the API key in
chrome.storage.sync
or
chrome.storage.local

, not in the page or hardcoded source.
- Keep API calls in the background service worker, not in the content script.
- Limit content script extraction to what is necessary, such as current selection or cleaned article text.
- Validate response shape before rendering.
- Handle missing keys, quota failures, and network errors in the popup or injected UI.
- Be explicit with permissions in
manifest.json
so the extension requests only what it needs.
A practical rule is simple: the page-facing script should never know the raw API key, and the service worker should never trust arbitrary page content without scoping or sanitization.
Comparison with alternatives

Compared with a backend-proxy architecture, the GovTranslate pattern reduces developer liability and infrastructure complexity because the developer’s server never receives the user’s prompts or outputs. Compared with putting API calls in a content script, it is safer because the service worker is a better place for credentials and cross-component orchestration.

Compared with full Playwright or Puppeteer browser automation, this extension pattern is lighter and more user-centric. It is ideal when the goal is to assist a human browsing session, while Playwright/Puppeteer is better when the goal is unattended automation or testing.
Key takeaways
A GovTranslate-style Claude extension usually means
direct browser-to-Anthropic calls with no custom backend
The
background service worker
should own API access and secret handling.
The
content script
should gather page context, not hold credentials.
Anthropic’s browser-call support depends on the
anthropic-dangerous-direct-browser-access: true
header.
This is a strong pattern for privacy-first translation and similar assistant features in Chrome extensions.
How It Works in Practice
Understanding the internals of calling claude api from a chrome extension (govtranslate pattern) helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Best Practices
Production experience with calling claude api from a chrome extension (govtranslate pattern) has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying calling claude api from a chrome extension (govtranslate pattern) in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
Summary

Calling Claude API from a Chrome extension (GovTranslate pattern) is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Summary

Calling Claude API from a Chrome extension (GovTranslate pattern) is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
Content Script Message Passing

The Chrome extension message passing system is the backbone of the GovTranslate architecture. Content scripts and service workers live in separate JavaScript contexts with no shared memory, so every piece of information must be explicitly serialized and passed through the chrome.runtime messaging API. Understanding this boundary precisely is essential to building a reliable extension because errors in message handling are among the most common causes of silent failures in Chrome extensions.

Messages are structured JSON objects that travel from one extension context to another. The content script initiates a request with chrome.runtime.sendMessage and waits asynchronously for the service worker to respond. The service worker registers a listener with chrome.runtime.onMessage.addListener that processes incoming messages and calls sendResponse with results. One critical nuance is that sendResponse is only valid synchronously unless the listener returns true to signal that it will respond asynchronously, which is always required when making awaited network calls inside the listener.
Always return true from the onMessage listener when using async/await to keep the message channel open until sendResponse is called
Use a type discriminator field like { type: 'TRANSLATE_SELECTION' } so one listener can handle multiple message kinds
For streaming responses or large data, use chrome.runtime.connect to open a long-lived Port instead of one-shot messages
Validate message shape in the service worker before acting on it to avoid crashes from malformed content script messages
Use chrome.tabs.sendMessage when the service worker needs to push a result back to a specific tab's content script
Keep message payloads small and serializable — DOM nodes, functions, and class instances cannot be transferred across the boundary
Handle the case where chrome.runtime.lastError is set after sendMessage to detect disconnected receivers gracefully
Use structured message types with TypeScript interfaces to catch schema mismatches at development time
